items.title: SWE-Bench Pro: Can AI Agents Solve Long-Horizon Software Engineering Tasks?
items.displayTitle: SWE-Bench Pro: Can AI Agents Solve Long-Horizon Software Engineering Tasks?
items.summary: SWE-Bench Pro argues that current agent evaluation has been too concentrated on lighter bug-fix settings and introduces a larger benchmark aimed at materially longer-horizon software engineering work: 1,865 problems from 41 actively maintained repositories, with tasks that often…

items.title: ABC-Bench: Benchmarking Agentic Backend Coding in Real-World Development
items.displayTitle: ABC-Bench: Benchmarking Agentic Backend Coding in Real-World Development
items.summary: ABC-Bench argues that backend coding agents should be judged on executable end-to-end work rather than isolated code edits. The benchmark is built around a full backend workflow, from repository exploration through deployment and external API-level validation, and the reported r…

items.title: FeatureBench: Benchmarking Agentic Coding for Complex Feature Development
items.displayTitle: FeatureBench: Benchmarking Agentic Coding for Complex Feature Development
items.summary: FeatureBench argues that current coding-agent evaluation is materially easier than real feature development and introduces a larger execution-based benchmark aimed at feature-level work that spans broader repository context, longer specifications, and multi-file implementation d…

items.title: COMPASS: Enhancing Agent Long-Horizon Reasoning with Evolving Context
items.displayTitle: COMPASS: Enhancing Agent Long-Horizon Reasoning with Evolving Context
items.summary: COMPASS argues that long-horizon agent performance is bottlenecked less by raw model capability than by context handling over extended tasks. Its answer is a hierarchical design that splits execution, planning, and context evolution across a MainAgent, a Meta-Thinker, and a Cont…

items.title: SWE-EVO: Benchmarking Coding Agents in Long-Horizon Software Evolution Scenarios
items.displayTitle: SWE-EVO: Benchmarking Coding Agents in Long-Horizon Software Evolution Scenarios
items.summary: SWE-EVO is a deliberately harder benchmark for coding agents: instead of short bug-fix tasks, it asks models to implement release-note changes inside full pre-release codebases, then grades them under strict no-regression rules. On that setup, strong agents that score well on SW…

items.title: How AI Coding Agents Modify Code: A Large-Scale Study of GitHub Pull Requests
items.displayTitle: How AI Coding Agents Modify Code: A Large-Scale Study of GitHub Pull Requests
items.summary: In this filtered comparison, pull requests produced by AI coding agents are not just marginal variants of human pull requests. They differ across every reported structural metric, with the sharpest separation in commit count, and they also show higher average description-to-diff…

items.title: Long-horizon agents: OpenCode + GPT-5.2 Codex Experiment
items.displayTitle: Long-horizon agents: OpenCode + GPT-5.2 Codex Experiment
items.summary: A bounded coding experiment reports that a main orchestrator dialogue can delegate implementation work to sub-agents, then verify and integrate their outputs, allowing a relatively large amount of total work to be coordinated through a much smaller main-thread context while comp…

items.title: NL2Repo-Bench: Towards Long-Horizon Repository Generation Evaluation of Coding Agents
items.displayTitle: NL2Repo-Bench: Towards Long-Horizon Repository Generation Evaluation of Coding Agents
items.summary: NL2Repo-Bench tests a harder coding-agent problem than patch-level benchmarks: given only a natural-language requirements document and an empty workspace, the agent must build a fully installable Python library. On this setup, long-horizon repository generation is still not solv…

items.title: Diagnosing Retrieval vs. Utilization Bottlenecks in LLM Agent Memory
items.displayTitle: Diagnosing Retrieval vs. Utilization Bottlenecks in LLM Agent Memory
items.summary: The paper argues that memory-augmented LLM agents are constrained more by getting the right memory back than by sophisticated memory writing or downstream use of already retrieved material. Its main contribution is a diagnostic evaluation that separates retrieval quality, utiliz…

items.title: AgentOrchestra: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework for General-Purpose Task Solving
items.displayTitle: AgentOrchestra: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework for General-Purpose Task Solving
items.summary: AgentOrchestra argues that a hierarchical multi-agent system can improve general-purpose task execution by splitting work across a planning layer and specialized execution agents, then revising the plan as intermediate results arrive. The paper supports that this design can prod…

items.title: Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents
items.displayTitle: Incident postmortem in the age of AI agents
items.summary: Firetiger’s ingest outage was not a single-point failure but a chained operational failure: a CI race condition and deploy attribution error wrote a non-existent container ID into deployment state, partial deploy behavior left infrastructure inconsistently mutated instead of saf…

items.title: On the Use of Agentic Coding: An Empirical Study of Pull Requests on GitHub
items.displayTitle: On the Use of Agentic Coding: An Empirical Study of Pull Requests on GitHub
items.summary: In this matched study of GitHub pull requests explicitly labeled as generated with Claude Code, agentic coding produced a high merge rate but still underperformed matched human pull requests on acceptance: 475 of 567 agentic pull requests were merged, an 83.77425% acceptance rat…

items.title: Where Do AI Coding Agents Fail? An Empirical Study of Failed Agentic Pull Requests in GitHub
items.displayTitle: Where Do AI Coding Agents Fail? An Empirical Study of Failed Agentic Pull Requests in GitHub
items.summary: AI coding agents are already capable of landing a large share of pull requests in public GitHub workflows, but success is uneven across agents and task types, and non-merge risk rises sharply when changes hit hard quality gates, especially CI. In this dataset, agentic coding wor…

items.title: What are popular AI coding benchmarks actually measuring? - nilenso blog
items.displayTitle: What are popular AI coding benchmarks actually measuring? - nilenso blog
items.summary: SWE-bench Verified is best read as a narrow test of whether an agent can produce a patch for a real GitHub issue that makes the issue’s unit tests pass. The source argues that this is useful but easy to overread: the benchmark is entirely Python, heavily concentrated in Django,…

items.title: #1 open-source agent on SWE-Bench Verified by combining Claude 3.7 and O1 | Augment Code
items.displayTitle: #1 open-source agent on SWE-Bench Verified by combining Claude 3.7 and O1 | Augment Code
items.summary: Augment’s report argues that a high SWE-bench Verified score can be pushed upward by system composition rather than by a single model alone: its submitted setup reportedly reached 65.4% by using Claude Sonnet 3.7 as the main agent and OpenAI o1 as an ensembler, while also inferr…

items.title: Testing AI coding agents (2025): Cursor vs. Claude, OpenAI, and Gemini | Render Blog
items.displayTitle: Testing AI coding agents (2025): Cursor vs. Claude, OpenAI, and Gemini | Render Blog
items.summary: This benchmark points to a narrow but useful conclusion: Cursor looked strongest in the greenfield app-generation test and also performed well on one production backend refactor, but the benchmark as a whole does not justify a clean overall ranking because part of the production…

items.title: Top Coding Agents (2025) | Benched.ai
items.displayTitle: Top Coding Agents (2025) | Benched.ai
items.summary: This source’s strongest usable contribution is not a clean leaderboard but a bounded reporting comparison: among the agents covered in the excerpt, Claude Code is the only one presented with multiple named benchmark figures across different evaluations and model variants, while…

items.title: SWE Context Bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning in Coding
items.displayTitle: SWE Context Bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning in Coding
items.summary: SWE-ContextBench reframes coding-agent evaluation around context reuse rather than isolated task solving by pairing 300 SWE-Bench Lite tasks with 99 manually verified related tasks from real issue and pull-request relationships. In the reported benchmark results, compact high-qu…

items.title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models
items.displayTitle: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models
items.summary: Agentic Context Engineering (ACE) argues that adaptation can be moved into context itself rather than into model weights or heavy supervised pipelines. In the paper’s reported evaluations, that approach produces meaningful benchmark gains across agent and finance tasks, includin…

items.title: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
items.displayTitle: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
items.summary: Software-engineering agents improve materially when the model is not left to operate through a raw shell alone but is given a language-model-tailored interface for search, file viewing, and guarded editing. In this paper, that interface raises benchmark solve rates versus prior…

items.title: Confucius Code Agent: An Open-sourced AI Software Engineer at Industrial Scale
items.displayTitle: Confucius Code Agent: An Open-sourced AI Software Engineer at Industrial Scale
items.summary: Confucius Code Agent presents an open-source software-engineering agent stack aimed at large-repository, industrial-style work by combining a unified orchestrator with hierarchical context management, persistent note reuse, and modular tool extensions, and the paper reports that…

items.title: How to Build AI Agents by Augmenting LLMs with Codified Human Expert Domain Knowledge? A Software Engineering Framework
items.displayTitle: How to Build AI Agents by Augmenting LLMs with Codified Human Expert Domain Knowledge? A Software Engineering Framework
items.summary: The paper argues that domain agents improve materially when an LLM is not left to improvise alone, but is coupled to explicit expert knowledge encoded as software, retrieval-backed code generation, and expert-guided design rules. In the reported evaluation, that combined archite…

items.title: Decoding the Configuration of AI Coding Agents: Insights from Claude Code Projects
items.displayTitle: Decoding the Configuration of AI Coding Agents: Insights from Claude Code Projects
items.summary: Public Claude Code project configurations are not lightweight prompt scraps; in the sampled repositories they function mainly as structured project orientation layers. The dominant pattern is to tell the coding agent how the system is organized, what it depends on, and what the…

items.title: Agentic Much? Adoption of Coding Agents on GitHub
items.displayTitle: Agentic Much? Adoption of Coding Agents on GitHub
items.summary: Coding-agent use on GitHub is already material rather than marginal, with the study estimating adoption at 15.85% to 22.60% across 129,134 projects. The central finding is not just that agent use is visible at scale, but that visible file traces alone miss a meaningful share of…

items.title: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development
items.displayTitle: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development
items.summary: ChatDev argues that software generation can be organized as a staged multi-agent process rather than a single-pass coding prompt: specialized agents negotiate design, implementation, review, and testing through structured dialogue, and that communication scaffold produces better…

items.title: An Empirical Study of Developer-Provided Context for AI Coding Assistants in Open-Source Projects
items.displayTitle: An Empirical Study of Developer-Provided Context for AI Coding Assistants in Open-Source Projects
items.summary: Developer-supplied context for AI coding assistants in open-source repositories is not a narrow set of prompts but a structured operating layer: across 401 cleaned repositories, maintainers most often supplied guidelines, project information, and conventions, usually in combinat…

items.title: Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control: AI Agent Use for Coding in 2025
items.displayTitle: Professional Software Developers Don’t Vibe, They Control: AI Agent Use for Coding in 2025
items.summary: Among experienced developers, AI coding agents are treated as supervised productivity tools rather than autonomous replacements. The paper’s central finding is not that professionals hand work over to agents, but that they preserve control through planning, close supervision, ou…

items.title: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024
items.displayTitle: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024
items.summary: SWE-bench turns real GitHub maintenance episodes into a tightly filtered executable benchmark, and the result is a hard test of practical code-repair ability: from roughly 90,000 pull requests across 12 Python repositories, only 2,294 tasks survive the admission pipeline, and un…

items.title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
items.displayTitle: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
items.summary: MetaGPT argues that code-generation performance improves when multi-agent software work is forced through explicit role specialization, structured intermediate artifacts, and executable feedback loops rather than left as an unstructured conversation. The reported result is stron…

items.title: Context Engineering for Multi-Agent LLM Code Assistants Using Elicit, NotebookLM, ChatGPT, and Claude Code
items.displayTitle: Context Engineering for Multi-Agent LLM Code Assistants Using Elicit, NotebookLM, ChatGPT, and Claude Code
items.summary: A context-engineered multi-agent code assistant can outperform a single-agent baseline on complex repository tasks by translating intent, retrieving outside and in-repo context, synthesizing that material into usable working knowledge, and routing implementation through speciali…

items.title: Context Engineering for AI Agents in Open-Source Software
items.displayTitle: Context Engineering for AI Agents in Open-Source Software
items.summary: Open-source repositories had low visible uptake of the agent-configuration formats this study examined, and the instruction files that do exist do not yet look like a settled engineering standard. Adoption was sparse in the sampled repositories, structure in AGENTS.md files was…

items.title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
items.displayTitle: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
items.summary: Evaluated multi-agent LLM systems show only marginal gains over single-agent baselines, and the weakness is not explained by one dominant defect. The paper’s central contribution is a failure landscape: 14 distinct failure modes across 3 categories, with evidence that coordinati…

items.title: Codified Context: Infrastructure for AI Agents in a Complex Codebase
items.displayTitle: Codified Context: Infrastructure for AI Agents in a Complex Codebase
items.summary: The paper argues that AI-agent work in a large live codebase can be stabilized by splitting project context into tiers with different loading rules: a permanently loaded constitution for operating rules and routing, task-invoked specialist agents for focused domain behavior, and…

items.title: When AGENTS.md Backfires: What a New Study Says About Context Files and Coding Agents
items.displayTitle: When AGENTS.md Backfires: What a New Study Says About Context Files and Coding Agents
items.summary: The reported ETH Zurich results cut against the default assumption that adding a context file helps coding agents. Across four agents and three context-file conditions, LLM-generated files more often reduced task success than improved it, while both generated and developer-writt…

items.title: arXiv 2601.20404
items.summary: In a paired study covering 10 repositories and 124 pull requests, the presence of repository-level AGENTS.md guidance was associated with faster agent completion and lower output-token use, but the paper supports an efficiency reading more strongly than a correctness reading bec…

items.title: Agent READMEs: An Empirical Study of Context Files for Agentic Coding
items.displayTitle: Agent READMEs: An Empirical Study of Context Files for Agentic Coding
items.summary: Agent context files are not marginal setup artifacts. Across a large cross-repository sample, they appear to function as active operational guidance for coding agents: they differ meaningfully by ecosystem, they are revised as living documents rather than written once, and their…

items.title: Testing and Enhancing Multi-Agent Systems for Robust Code Generation
items.displayTitle: Testing and Enhancing Multi-Agent Systems for Robust Code Generation
items.summary: Current code-generation multi-agent systems are materially less robust than headline solve rates suggest: when prompts are rewritten in meaning-preserving ways, systems that had already solved a problem newly fail on 7.9% to 83.3% of those same problems. The paper’s main practic…

items.title: Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
items.displayTitle: Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
items.summary: Scaling agent systems is not a general recipe for better performance. In the studied setting, multi-agent gains are conditional, benchmark-specific, and often erased by coordination cost once the base model is already fairly competent or the task is tool-heavy. The paper’s main…

items.title: Repository-level context files mostly add cost and activity overhead rather than improving coding-agent success
items.displayTitle: Repository-level context files mostly add cost and activity overhead rather than improving coding-agent success
items.summary: This study finds that repository-level context files, as currently used, usually do not help coding agents solve software tasks better. Across the evaluated agents and models, they generally lowered success rates relative to giving no repository context while raising inference c…
